EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression 02W developed about 375 miles east-southeast of Yap early on the morning of March 22nd. Moving west-northwest at about 15 mph, TD 02W   intensified into Tropical Storm Omais by 0400 GST the morning of the 23rd as it was passing southwest of the island of Fais in western Yap State. Omais maintained minimal tropical storm force winds of 40 mph as it continued moving west-northwest, passing between Ulithi and Yap around 1100 GST on the 23rd. Closest points of approach were 40 miles southwest of Fais at 0500 GST, 25 miles southwest of Ulithi at 1000 GST, and 60 miles northeast of Yap at 1900 GST. By the afternoon of the 24th, Tropical Storm Omais was over 250 miles northwest of Yap, still packing winds of 40 mph. ||Tropical Storm Omais brought several hours of tropical storm conditions to Ulithi and Fais, while Yap was affected relatively little. At Fais, winds were estimated at 50 to 60 mph during the early morning hours of the 23rd, while at Ulithi winds were estimated at 35 to 45 mph. At Yap International Airport, the maximum sustained wind was only 23 mph from the north from 0700 to 1100 GST, and the peak gust was from the north at 40 mph at 0757 GST. Unofficial reports indicated somewhat higher winds north of the airport and Colonia, where some tree branches and coconuts were blown down. The lowest sea-level pressure at the airport was 1001.2 mb at 1552 GST. Ulithi reported 3.98 inches of rain on the 23rd, and Fais likely had a similar amount. But Yap was on the dry side of the storm, receiving only 0.22 inches of rain. ||The only island to report any significant damage was Fais, where 70% of the papaya, banana, and breadfruit were damaged or destroyed. Crop damage is estimated at $10,000 for Fais (two-thirds) and Ulithi (one-third).

About 2230 GST the evening of April 21st, three men in their 30s who were spear fishing off the east coast of Guam in Togcha Bay near Talofofo were caught in and separated by strong rip currents. One of the men made it to shore and called for help, but the other two were pulled under and drowned. After an extensive search over the next few days, only one body was recovered.

At about 0400 GST on the 21st of September, Tropical Depression 13W intensified into Tropical Storm Malakas sbout 60 miles northwest of Agrihan and 90 miles northwest of Pagan Island in the Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands. Tropical Storm Malakas moved erratically during the day about 50-70 miles northwest and west of the two islands...then moved off to the west-northwest that evening and overnight. No wind measurements are available from either island, but satellite data and data from Air Force reconnaissance aircraft indicate that winds up to 40 mph likely affected both Agrihan and Pagan during the day on the 21st. No damage or injuries were reported.
